denial_pattern_sources: mhpaea_visit_limit_asymmetry: MHPAEA Final Rule 2024; KFF 2023 Mental Health Parity Enforcement Report
contraindication_to_step_therapy: AMA 2023 Prior Authorization Physician Survey; State step-therapy exception laws (NY, TX, IL)
denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter confirms the outcome of our review of the request for In-lab polysomnography related to Obstructive sleep apnea, moderate (G47.33).

After clinical review using Cigna Medical Coverage Policies and MCG Care Guidelines, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: Home sleep apnea test required first-line. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria for prior authorization determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Insurer demands a cheaper modality (HSAT vs in-lab sleep study; oral vs infusion).

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize In-lab polysomnography. If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

Cigna Health Care Management
clinical_context:
The 24-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Obstructive sleep apnea, moderate (G47.33). He has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended In-lab polysomnography as the next appropriate step.

BMI 38, CHF, and suspected central apneas. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on modality substitution without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.
